messages.content:
Why do I always get this error in my rust flashloan code?  --> /Users/robertmccormick/.cargo/registry/src/github.com-1ecc6299db9ec823/ethers-core-2.0.0/src/types/i256.rs:128:14
    |
128 |         Self(U256::one())
    |              ^^^^^^^^^^^
    |
    = note: calls in constant functions are limited to constant functions, tuple structs and tuple variants

The error message is indicating that you are trying to call a non-constant function in a constant function. In this case, you are trying to create a new `U256` instance using `U256::one()`, which is not a constant function. 

To resolve this error, you should check if the function that is calling `U256::one()` is marked as a constant function or not. If it is not, you should consider marking it as a constant function. If that is not possible, you should consider changing the way you are creating the `U256` instance to use a constant function or a tuple struct/variant.
please retrieve the one function from : https://github.com/gnosis/ethcontract-rs/
please retrieve the one struct from : https://github.com/gnosis/ethcontract-rs/
where is the zero function in : https://github.com/gnosis/ethcontract-rs/
Please explain this error in my rust code: error[E0015]: cannot call non-const fn `U256::is_zero` in constant functions

I keep getting this error:  cannot call non-const fn `U256::one` in constant functions
   -->  cargo/registry/src/github.com-1ecc6299db9ec823/ethers-core-2.0.0/src/types/i256.rs:128:14
I keep getting this error is my ethers-core library for my rust code :  cannot call non-const fn `U256::one` in constant functions

This error occurs because you are calling the non-const function `U256::one()` within a const context, such as in a `const` declaration or in a function that is marked as `const`.

To resolve this error, you have a few options:

1. Replace `U256::one()` with a constant expression. You can create a `const` instance of `U256` that has the same value as `U256::one()`.

```rust
const MY_U256_ONE: U256 = U256([1, 0, 0, 0]);
```

Note that this may not be practical if you need to use `U256::one()` in many places across your code.

2. Mark your function as non-const if possible. If your function does not truly require a `const` context (for example, if you are not trying to generate a `const` value), then you can remove the `const` modifier from your function. This may not be practical if you are relying on this function to generate a `const` value that is used elsewhere in your code.

3. Create a wrapper function that is `const`. You can create a function that returns the value of `U256::one()`, and mark that function as `const`. You can then call this function within your `const` functions or expressions.

```rust
const fn get_u256_one() -> U256 {
    U256([1, 0, 0, 0])
}
```

Then within your `const` functions or expressions:

```rust
let my_u256_one = get_u256_one();
```

This approach allows you to use a `const` function to generate the value of `U256::one()` in a `const` context. However, note that the `const` function cannot depend on any non-const values or functions.

cannot call non-const fn `U256::is_zero` in constant functions
   --> /Users/robertmccormick/.cargo/registry/src/github.com-1ecc6299db9ec823/ethers-core-2.0.0/src/types/i256.rs:279:16
    |
279 |         self.0.is_zero()
    |                ^^^^^^^^^
